aliases: Grilled Eel and Seafood Sushi Bento

english: Grilled Eel and Seafood Sushi Bento

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi class (FOODON_03315595); no specific cooked sushi bento subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers sushi as a category; no specific cooked-only or bento-format subcode exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked sushi bento as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cooked sushi bento box is approximately 300 g, containing an assortment of nigiri and maki pieces with grilled eel, tamagoyaki, shrimp, crabstick, and cooked scallop over seasoned sushi rice.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Sushi Bento — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Cooked Sushi Bento (cr_id G00010469). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous mixed sushi dish (USDA SR Legacy #35188 'Sushi, with fish') as a conservative proxy and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cooked sushi bento as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant ingredient: white sushi rice (short-grain Japanese rice, vinegar-seasoned). The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 72 (±8) for white short-grain Japanese rice (Koshihikari-type, cooked). The seafood and egg components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 55.17) / 100 ≈ 39.7, rounded to 39.8, using 300 g serving size and 18.39 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 428 mg/100 g with low potassium (48 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g), providing insufficient compensatory nutrients to offset sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal, avoid additional soy sauce dipping, and use pickled ginger sparingly, as sodium intake from a standard bento box can exceed 1,280 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) driven by white short-grain sushi rice with added sugar in vinegar seasoning, combined with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) that is insufficient to moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to 100–150 g, request brown rice sushi where available, and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose spike from the high-GI sushi rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (428 mg/100 g) raises cardiovascular risk; however, total fat is low (0.67 g/100 g) and cholesterol is low (4 mg/100 g), partially mitigating lipid-related cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 150–200 g, strictly avoid additional soy sauce to keep sodium below 600 mg per meal, and choose sushi fillings with omega-3-rich fish such as salmon or mackerel when available to support cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 428 mg/100 g may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling; cooked seafood fillings (eel, shrimp, scallop, crabstick) provide some protein but limited confirmed omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit compared to raw fatty fish.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise soy sauce use to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling and request omega-3-rich fillings such as salmon or mackerel to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked Sushi Bento is a Japanese-style bento box meal consisting of an assortment of cooked seafood sushi pieces served over vinegar-seasoned short-grain white rice. Typical fillings include grilled eel (unagi), tamagoyaki (sweet Japanese rolled omelette), cooked shrimp nigiri, crabstick (surimi), and cooked scallop. Unlike traditional raw sushi, all protein components in this bento are heat-treated, making it suitable for consumers who avoid raw fish. The rice base is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a mildly sweet and tangy flavour. The overall taste profile is savoury-sweet with soft, slightly sticky rice texture and tender seafood toppings. Per 100 g, the dish provides 94 kcal, 2.92 g protein, 0.67 g fat, and 18.39 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 428 mg — a clinically significant level. The glycemic index is estimated at 72 (high), driven by the white sushi rice base. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0 g/100 g. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to high sodium and low potassium offset, and unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to high GI and low fibre. Cardiovascular risk is moderate given the sodium load despite low total fat. Commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore Japanese food outlets and supermarket ready-meal sections. Allergens include fish, shellfish, egg, wheat (from surimi/crabstick), and soy.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010469, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous sushi entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for white short-grain Japanese rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite cooked sushi bento dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010469) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite cooked sushi bento as of 2026. Saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy #35188 'Sushi, with fish' as the closest approved-source analogue; this is a conservative proxy and should be updated if HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white short-grain Japanese rice (Koshihikari-type) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cooked sushi bento as a composite dish exists in the approved GI source database. All three core macros (protein_g 2.92, fat_g 0.67, carbohydrate_g 18.39) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required. Halal status is unconfirmed as eel and certain seafood preparations may not meet halal certification requirements depending on preparation facility.
